summary:
High-risk regulatory changes for food seasoning/snack compliance:

*   **GCC/GSO:** New mandatory allergen declaration requirements are expected (GSO 9:2022), requiring verification of specific lists and rules.
*   **Japan:** New prohibitions on additives (unless MHLW specifies harmless), strict limits on agricultural chemical/feed additive/animal pharmaceutical residues, and a ban on toxic/harmful substances or pathogen contamination if likely to cause harm. Verification against MHLW specifications is crucial.
*   **Singapore:** New rules mandate that all food additives must be SFA-assessed and permitted, with non-permitted additives prohibited. Contaminants must be within SFA limits, with exceeding limits leading to prohibition. Specific residues (Diethylstibestrol, Hexoestrol, Dienoestrol) are prohibited in meat products. Labelling now explicitly requires declaration of all ingredients/additives and general compliance.
*   **Taiwan:** Mandatory allergen declaration for specific items (Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame, milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten, soybeans, fish) and sulphite (≥10 mg/kg) is reinforced.
*   **Thailand:** New compliance required for general food and nutrition labeling.
*   **United States:** Prohibited substances are explicitly not permitted. Sesame is now a major food allergen (effective Jan 1, 2023), requiring declaration. New action levels for total aflatoxins (20 ppb) in general foods and peanut products. 'Gluten-free' claims have a defined standard, including for fermented/hydrolyzed foods. Mandatory written procedures for allergen preventive controls are required. Cochineal extract, carmine, and FD&C Yellow No. 5 require mandatory label declaration. Specific methods for major food allergen declaration (parentheses or 'contains' statement) are mandatory, including declaring the type/species for tree nuts, fish, and Crustacean shellfish. Sulfiting agents must be declared if ≥10 ppm total sulfites.

changes.notes: As a general labelling regulation for prepackaged foods, GSO 9:2022 is expected to include requirements for the declaration of allergens. The specific list of allergens and declaration rules must be verified within the full text of the regulation.

changes.parameter: Additives (excluding natural flavoring agents and generally consumed substances used as additives)
changes.newLimit: Prohibited unless the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare specifies them as unlikely to cause harm to human health. Verification required.
changes.notes: Specific additives and their permitted uses must be verified against MHLW specifications.

changes.parameter: Residues of agricultural chemicals, feed additives, and animal pharmaceuticals
changes.newLimit: Must not exceed quantities specified by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare as unlikely to cause harm to human health, unless specific residual limits are established. Verification required.
changes.notes: Specific residual limits for agricultural chemicals, feed additives, and animal pharmaceuticals must be verified against MHLW specifications (Positive List System).

changes.parameter: Toxic or harmful substances, foreign substances
changes.newLimit: Prohibited if containing or bearing toxic or harmful substances (unless MHLW specifies harmless) or likely to cause harm due to contamination or foreign substances.
Prohibited if contaminated with pathogens and likely to cause harm to human health.
Must be assessed and permitted by SFA
changes.notes: Only SFA-assessed and permitted food additives are allowed for use in food products sold in Singapore.
If a food product contains an additive that is not permitted, it must not be imported or sold.

changes.newLimit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
changes.notes: Contaminants in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
Food products containing contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.
Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of these compounds.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared on food labels
changes.notes: All ingredients and additives used in prepacked food products for sale in Singapore must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.
Prepacked food products must comply with general labelling requirements.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.notes: Mandatory labeling is required if these allergens are present as an ingredient or food additive in prepackaged foods, including seasoning and snack products.
Mandatory declaration is required if sulphite as residue sulphur dioxide is present at 10 mg/kg or more in prepackaged foods, including seasoning and snack products.
Food products must adhere to general food labeling and specific nutrition labeling requirements.
Substances listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food are not permitted.

changes.oldLimit: Milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, sesame
changes.newLimit: Milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
changes.notes: These nine foods are identified by law as major food allergens in the U.S. The addition of sesame was effective January 1, 2023.
This is an Action Level for total aflatoxins in general foods.
This is an Action Level for total aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut products.
The FDA has a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling, including compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods (effective 08/12/2020).
Facilities must put written procedures in place to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per the CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117).
Cochineal extract and carmine must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.
Foods or ingredients containing a 'major food allergen' must be specifically labeled with the name of the allergen source.
All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels.

changes.parameter: Method of major food allergen declaration
changes.notes: The allergen's food source must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name OR immediately after/next to the ingredient list in a 'contains' statement.

changes.parameter: Specific declaration for tree nuts, fish, and Crustacean shellfish
changes.notes: The type of tree nut, and the species of fish and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food at a concentration of 10 parts per million or more total sulfites.

changes.oldLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
changes.newLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database (Codex STAN 192-1995)
changes.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. Users are encouraged to consult the database and its Preamble for specific provisions.

changes.oldLimit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and CCCF established levels
changes.newLimit: Verification required via General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995)
changes.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants in food and feed commodities to protect human health, based on sound scientific evidence. The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses these levels.

changes.oldLimit: Follow Code of Practice for prevention and reduction
changes.newLimit: Follow Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices (CXC 78-2017)
changes.notes: This Code of Practice provides guidance for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxin contamination specifically in spices.

changes.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
changes.notes: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, and process hygiene criteria for foods placed on the market.

changes.parameter: Foodborne bacteria (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii), their toxins and metabolites (e.g., staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
changes.notes: Rule no longer appeared in the latest extraction. Verify whether source changed or extraction missed it.

changes.parameter: General labelling requirements for prepackaged foodstuffs
changes.notes: This technical regulation sets out the general requirements for the labelling of all prepackaged foodstuffs. Specific details regarding mandatory information, format, and language must be verified within the full text of the regulation.

GSO 9:2022, as a general labelling regulation, is expected to cover nutritional information, including the declaration of salt or sodium content. Specific requirements for declaration and any potential limits or guidelines must be verified within the full text of the regulation.

changes.parameter: Halal compliance for ingredients and additives
changes.notes: This technical regulation outlines the general requirements for Halal food, which includes criteria for ingredients and processing, indirectly impacting additive choices.
This technical regulation specifies the general requirements for labelling of prepackaged food products.

changes.newLimit: Not required by law, must be truthful and not misleading
changes.notes: Advisory statements like 'may contain [allergen]' are not legally required and should only be used to address unavoidable cross-contact after good manufacturing practices have been implemented.
